utterances: Historically, to be able to sell a show in reruns, networks only had to know one thing...
Great comedy works.

Brad Siegel is the former president of Turner Entertainment Networks, and under his watch, TNT stole - or really outbid - A&E for reruns of ""Law & Order."" And the other unwritten rule, Siegel says, used to be that shows had to have at least 66 episodes under their belt.
That magic number produced a bigger profit when broadcast networks auctioned their shows off, because it allows a cable channel to do what the industry calls stripping.
To be able to run a show Monday through Friday in a certain time slot.
And typically, you'd want to go three months before you started to repeat.
There used to be big bucks to be made by selling a show off its original network as a rerun.

HBO sold ""The Sopranos"" to A&E for more than $2 million per episode, and that's roughly the same amount that CBS got from TNT for reruns of ""The Mentalist."" But today, any rules that regulated the rerun market are being scrambled.
Cable channels are replacing reruns with their own original programming, and that's crowding out reruns.
Derek Kompare is the author of ""Rerun Nation: How Repeats Invented American Television.
These days, there's certainly more cachet in developing original series, and I think ultimately, they're seeking more profits for that, too.
Original shows like ""Royal Pains"" on USA, ""The Closer"" on TNT and ""Mad Men"" on AMC have replaced reruns as the backbone of their networks.
Again, Brad Siegel.
Cable networks have tried to become brands.
And in that brand and in the way they define themselves, they create certain filters on the kinds of shows that, when you put them all together, they sort of define a network.
Only on TBS.
Very funny.

So if TBS is very funny, and characters are welcome on the USA Network and TNT knows drama, then what cable networks are willing to buy as a rerun now suddenly goes through a stiff filtering process, ensuring that rerun will get along well with what cable networks are deeming their first priorities: their original programs.
For NPR News, I'm Jesse Baker.
NPR's Frank Morris has that story.
Just off a boat ride through some of the hardest-hit marshes at the mouth of the Mississippi River, a rail-thin man in a black T-shirt and cap steps to the microphone.
What's really happening out there is nothing.
That's James Carville, former President Clinton's savvy strategist.
The silence that - you could've been in Antarctica.
There were no boats.
There were no activity.
There was no anything.
Governor Bobby Jindal, a rising star in the Republican Party, couldn't agree more.
What really bugs him is that he hasn't been able to get permission, let alone federal help, to build miles of linear sand piles just offshore - berms to block the advancing oil.

Every day we wait for this permit is another day of delay, is another day that this question has been answered for us that we're going to have to fight this oil in our wetlands instead of on these barrier islands.
For NPR News, I'm Frank Morris in Venice, Louisiana.
This is ALL THINGS CONSIDERED from NPR News.
I'm Melissa Block.
And I'm Lynn Neary.
Private companies, international aid experts and diplomats are gathered in Washington this week, all to help the world's newest nation get on its feet.
South Sudan's challenges are great.
It is one of the most underdeveloped places on Earth and it has many lingering disputes with its former rulers in Khartoum.
As NPR's Michele Kelemen reports, fresh fighting along the border between the two Sudans is making the U.S.
and other donors nervous.
Secretary of State Hillary Clinton compares South Sudan to a tiny infant in need of intensive care.
Yes, the work ahead is not quick nor easy, but neither was winning independence.
South Sudan defied the odds, simply by being born.
She kicked off the two-day conference talking about the challenges facing a country that has few paved roads and little in the way of infrastructure.
It does, though, have oil wealth and Clinton says that needs to be well managed.

We know that it will either help your country finance its own path out of poverty or you will fall prey to the natural resource curse, which will enrich a small elite, outside interests, corporations and countries, and leave your people hardly better off than when you started.
Teresa, that call, you were very young, but 6 years old, that obviously set out your career and your life since then.
Was it genuine?

I do feel, though, that one of the anxieties that is being unexpressed, or expressed in other venues, has to do with the possibility of an Obama loss and the notion that there could be rioting as a result of an Obama loss.

I mean, one of the things we have to be very clear about is that race is still part of the tinderbox of this entire election, that we do have this historic possibility of an African-American candidate.
Now I am confident that the people of Chicago, black and white, Latino and Asian, are completely uninterested in any kind of violence.
That this is a moment of pure expression of solidarity.
Whether that solidarity is in a win, or solidarity in a loss.
So I don't expect anything negative.
But I do think part of the national anxiety at this moment is the possibility, in this racialized moment, of some sort of violence.
And I really, again, believe that the people of Chicago are interested in being with one another, in either jubilation or in sorrow, but in solidarity, not in violence.
I happened to be there for the Chicago Bulls victory, where people were, you know, drunk and basically throwing things through windows.
Not a lot of them, you know, but enough of them to make a difference.
Are you concerned - that was something that was a victory.

Are you concerned that even in victory for some of these political supporters of Senator Obama, that there could just be issues because, you know, as the police chief mentioned, sometimes people don't have common sense?
I mean, you know, alcohol and crowds is always potentially troubling.
I mean, there's no doubt about that.

I will say that I have a great deal of confidence in Mayor Daley, and that if Mayor Daley thought his city was at all in danger, he would have been unlikely to say that this was an acceptable activity.
He's not a hands off mayor, that's for sure.

I think the other piece here is, to the extent that I have any worries at all around questions of wins and losses and violence, my bigger anxiety is actually about the possibility that some McCain supporters, those supporters who have done things like crash a car into a telephone pole in North Carolina trying to bring down electricity today, the woman, of course that we all know about, who carved a B into her face.
You know, there are some - certainly not even anywhere close to the majority, a tiny, tiny proportion who I worry about, but not in the city of Chicago.
That city is going to go vastly, predominantly for Obama.
I worry about places we're not thinking about at all where there might be sporadic acts of anxiety or violence in the wake of a potential Obama win.

Well Chief Timoney, when you think about any number of interactions - and specifically I'm thinking myself of some of the political conventions, and how there have been tensions between police and protestors or even police and journalists - how do you talk to your force, your police force, and say this is how you deal with a crowd, and this is how you get a crowd to calm down if it's getting a little uneasy?
Well, we've had, just in the last five years here, we've had violent protestors.
We've also had, just in my five years here, three major celebrations.
We've had the Heat win, and we've had the Marlins baseball team win twice, and we didn't have one incident in any one of those three events.
We've also had two big premature sightings of Castro's death, which saw an outpouring of thousands of people out on the streets.
And when people are celebrating, what you want to do is give them some room, cordon off the area, divert traffic.
But at the same time kind of monitor the, if you will, the temperature of the crowd.
You've got to really look in to make sure that there isn't an awful lot of drinking going on.
Particularly young kids, they're not allowed to drink anyway, so you should confiscate it.
And so if you do things like that - you divert traffic to go round where the celebration is taking place, you give the celebrants enough room to celebrate.
But at the same time, kind of monitor the crowd so that one or two knuckleheads - and that's all it takes.
You could have 99 and nine-tenths of the people behaving marvelously, but you've got two or three knuckleheads acting up and, you know, the rest, as they say, is history.
Melissa, do you have any perspective about how the police and the citizens interact in Chicago, or possibly any concerns about that interaction?
Well I mean, certainly Chicago, like many major cities, has a variety of anxieties.
Particularly around poor communities, African-American communities.
In Chicago very much on the South Side and the West Side.
But I will also say that over the past decade they have instituted community policing.
Very, very active community policing, so that the men and women who will be on the streets tonight are not strangers to the communities that they will be policing tonight.
They are known to those communities.
And again, you know, police and fire officials, they're citizens too.
They went out and voted today, and you know, in many ways I think, again, this is a matter of solidarity.
And that what Chicagoans are interested here in, in this moment, is representing their city in the best light.
If Chicago becomes the home city of the next president of the United States, it will want to usher in that experience with a sign of the greatness of the city, not with - not with violence.
Chief Timoney, what about tips for people who go out, not necessarily just in Chicago.
There are going to be other places where people gather to think about what this election has meant to them.
If you find yourself in a situation where you think the crowd may be a little unstable, or you feel unsafe, what are some tips for people as they're out and about?
No, listen.
There's an expectation in that there maybe spontaneous celebrations, and that's great.
There's nothing wrong with that.
God bless them all.
But in the event if, God forbid, a man or a woman with their child is out there just celebrating and see it start to turn ugly, the best thing to do is just get out of there.
Head back home and, you know, watch it on television.
What if you can't get out easily?
At the courthouse in Greenfield, Mass., after waiting about 20 minutes for their turn, Ashley Erhoe, her young children and their grandmother Michelle Howe enter the court room.
Howe's the one who had her daughter committed two years ago.
After everyone settles in, probate officer Linda Singer goes before Judge Beth Crawford.
Well that's - you know, find a police officer and say listen, I need to get out of here.
Hopefully, hopefully that none of this comes to pass.
I don't expect it myself.
I think there's a maturation process that's occurred in this country, and so I'm not expecting it.
I'm not expecting any untoward activities.
I'm expecting maybe some celebrations, and God bless them, we'll be there to celebrate with them.
But you know, if God forbid something did happen, then I see police departments all across America that would be prepared.
All right, Melissa.
Just quickly, how are you planning to watch the results, and/or to mark the evening?
Well, I will be in New York City.
I will be doing media and press.
It kind of depends on what time of night.
Depending on which results, but I'm certainly not going to sleep tonight.
And I will be marking it one amazing moment at a time.
Because no matter what, this is quite a historic election.
Well, on that note, Melissa and Chief Timoney, thank you so much.
